description: This figure presents a comparative set of optical micrographs showing the metallographic structure of Fe–Mn–Al–C austenitic low-density steels across four conditions: (a) M1-SS, (b) M1-30, (c) M2-SS, and (d) M2-30, with a scale bar of 50 μm applying to all images. The micrographs reveal equiaxed austenite grain morphologies in all samples. Visually, the M1 series ((a) and (b)) exhibits coarser grain structures compared to the Ti/V-modified M2 series ((c) and (d)), indicating a grain-refinement effect from microalloying. The caption identifies (a) as solution-treated M1, (b) as M1 aged at 550°C for 30 h, (c) as solution-treated M2, and (d) as M2 aged at 550°C for 30 h. According to the text, M1-SS shows a single-phase austenite matrix with an average grain size of 42.4 ± 7.8 μm, while M1-30 retains a similar grain size (~45.3 μm) but contains uniformly distributed intragranular κ-carbide precipitates not individually resolvable by optical microscopy. The M2 steel exhibits significantly finer grains (~32.3 μm in M2-SS and ~35.8 μm in M2-30) due to intragranular (Ti,V)C carbides; after aging, κ-carbides heterogeneously precipitate at the edges of these (Ti,V)C particles. Collectively, the figure demonstrates that Ti/V addition refines the austenite grain structure and alters the κ-carbide precipitation mode from uniform intragranular distribution to heterogeneous nucleation on pre-existing (Ti,V)C carbides.
